FlashQL

Conflict Handling

Sync Integration Patterns

Language Reference

Engineering

The Realtime Engine

On this page

Conflict Handling ​

FlashQL sync is designed around predictable conflict behavior.

That does not mean every conflict is magically merged. It means the system has a clear model for when a local write can still be applied and when it has lost the race to newer authoritative state.

Start with the intuition ​

If two clients both edit the same row while offline, you do not want the later reconnect to silently overwrite the first one.

You want the system to say, in effect:

"apply this write only if the origin row is still the version I think it is"

That is the heart of FlashQL's conflict model.

The role of upstream_mvcc_key ​

For replicated writeable views, the main view-level knob is upstream_mvcc_key.

Example:

sql
CREATE REALTIME VIEW public.users AS
SELECT * FROM public.users
WITH (
  replication_origin = 'postgres:primary',
  upstream_mvcc_key = 'XMIN'
);

This tells FlashQL which origin-side version column or token should be used to condition outbound writes.

What that means practically ​

Without an origin version token, an outbound update may only be able to say:

update row id = 1

With an origin version token, it can instead say:

update row id = 1 only if it is still version 13297

That difference is what makes conflict handling predictable.

Defaults ​

For PostgreSQL- and FlashQL-backed origins, FlashQL can often default upstream_mvcc_key to XMIN for updateable replicated views.

That gives a useful default conflict story without forcing every view definition to repeat it.

What a conflict usually means ​

A conflict usually means one of these:

  • another client already updated the row
  • another client already deleted the row
  • the origin row version no longer matches the local expectation

So conflicts are expected sync outcomes, not mysterious transport failures.

How this relates to write policy ​

Conflict detection works with both write policies, but the user experience differs.

origin_first ​

  • local writes queue outbound work
  • visible local state waits for authoritative inbound data
  • a conflicting outbound attempt can become conflicted
  • inbound still brings the local row to the authoritative state

local_first ​

  • local writes stage visible row state immediately
  • the row carries __staged = true
  • a conflicting outbound attempt can become conflicted
  • inbound later normalizes the row back to authoritative state

So in both models:

  • queue state tells you what happened to the outbound attempt
  • inbound origin data restores authoritative row state

The main conflict scenarios ​

These are the core cases to keep in mind.

update vs update ​

  • two offline clients both update the same row
  • the first reconnect to apply upstream wins
  • the second reconnect conflicts
  • inbound catches both clients up to the winner's state

delete vs update ​

  • one client deletes while offline
  • another updates the row and reconnects first
  • the later delete can conflict
  • inbound restores the updated authoritative row

update vs delete ​

  • one client updates while offline
  • another deletes the row and reconnects first
  • the later update can conflict
  • inbound removes the row locally

These cases have been exercised in real stack tests.

Observable conflict behavior ​

db.sync emits a dedicated conflict event for these cases.

That lets applications observe conflict as its own operational category instead of treating it as a generic network error.

Queue rows can also end up with:

  • status = 'conflicted'

which is useful for inspection and debugging.

What conflict handling is not claiming ​

FlashQL is not currently claiming:

  • arbitrary semantic merges
  • domain-specific reconciliation logic
  • that every possible view shape is safely writable

The strong claim is narrower and more useful:

  • when a replicated writable view has a usable origin version token, FlashQL can detect and classify write races predictably

Related docs ​

  • Federation, Materialization, Realtime Views, and Write Paths
  • FlashQL Sync
  • Sync Integration Patterns
